evals.prompt:
Review this serial port code:
```python
import serial

def send_command(cmd: str) -> str:
    port = serial.Serial('COM3', 9600)
    port.write(cmd.encode())
    response = port.read(1024)
    port.close()
    return response.decode()
```
evals.expected_output: Analysis: (1) Hardcoded 'COM3' — not portable, will fail on Linux/macOS; (2) No timeout set on Serial() — read(1024) will block indefinitely if device sends fewer than 1024 bytes; (3) No flow control (RTS/CTS or XON/XOFF) specified — may cause data loss at 9600 baud with slow devices; (4) Port not opened in a context manager — close() won't be called if an exception occurs mid-operation; (5) 1024-byte read assumes fixed response size — most serial protocols are line-terminated or length-prefixed.

assertions.text: Flags that port.close() won't run if an exception occurs (missing context manager)

evals.prompt: We're trying to communicate with a Modbus RTU device over RS-485 at 19200 baud, but we're seeing corrupted frames randomly. What should we check?
evals.expected_output: Systematic Modbus RTU debugging checklist: (1) RS-485 termination resistors at both ends of the bus (120 ohm); (2) Inter-frame silence gap — Modbus RTU requires 3.5 character times minimum between frames (~1.8ms at 19200); (3) TX/RX direction control — many USB-RS485 adapters need explicit RTS toggle for half-duplex; (4) Ground reference — RS-485 is differential but still needs common ground; (5) Check for 120-ohm termination at both ends only; (6) pymodbus or minimalmodbus library inter-frame gap setting.

assertions.text: Mentions the Modbus RTU 3.5 character inter-frame gap requirement
Mentions RTS direction control for half-duplex RS-485
